video.title: Breakaway Brilliance In The Mountains | Giro D'Italia 2022 Stage 17 Highlights

transcript.clean_text: On stage 16 of the Giro d'Italia, Jan Hirt could finally raise his arms. 5 years of trying to win a stage, he eventually did it. That in Aprica after the mighty Mortirolo, whereas Richard Carapaz came in on the same time as Jai Hindley, but bonus seconds meant that his lead in the GC was cut to three. The peloton turned up for stage 17. After 16 days of good weather, it was time for a change. Rain jackets dusted off, cold weather gear put on, had a big day in the saddle ahead. Once more, a trek into the mountains. Starting in Ponte di Legno and going over the Passo del Tonale to begin, a four-star stage with two first category climbs in the final 45 km. First, the Passo del Vetriolo, then the Monte Rovere, Salita del Menador, before the finish in Lavarone, just down the hill, 8 km after going over the final climb. 168 km in total, and it would be a fight for the breakaway on the first climb straight out of kilometer zero. The rain falling, and when Koen Bouwman went, Giulio Ciccone tried to join him. 25 riders ended up the road, and with an initial gap of around a minute and a half, things quickly started to grow out. The last two to join were David de la Cruz and Diego Rosa, the latter trying to resurrect his King of the Mountains bid. But, it would be Koen Bouwman who'd extend his lead in the competition, beginning with a 69-point lead on the day. He'd add nine points to that lead early on with the third category Giovo climb. Then a spill, 80 km to go, and Santiago Buitrago of Bahrain Victorious was in pain, down, but not out. He'd get a new bike, get back on, and rejoin the attackers. The attackers started to split each other up, and with 65 km and two first category climbs to go, cyclocrosser, mountain biker, classics man Mathieu van der Poel attacked. It was like something off a computer game. Van der Poel going out there in search of a win in the mountains, just like his fellow crosser Wout van Aert had done in the Tour de France the year before. Hugh Carthy was again at the front, as was Jan Hirt, the stage winner the day previous. They were chasing at about 20 seconds. Van der Poel had three mates up the road by then, and Giulio Ciccone will be spat out the back, perhaps paying for the efforts of previous days. On the penultimate climb, the weather was still inclement, but things changed over the top. The sun began to shine, and Koen Bouwman picked up 40 more points. The maglia azzurra, the king of the mountains competition, in his control. Over the top, both Mathieu van der Poel, who had a bit of a nervous moment, and Heislermayer attacked. They pulled out 30 seconds on the initial turns and twists of the descent, and by the valley, that gap had turned into a minute. Behind they were falling out with each other. 1 minute 32 seconds was the gap when Mathieu van der Poel took off on the final climb of the day, the Salita de Manador. Heislermayer was left back, and in the main GC group behind, following on at 5 minutes, Bahrain Victorious went to work with João Almeida showing the first signs of weakness. Hugh Carthy, the chasers at 1 minute 26, were left behind by 22-year-old Colombian Santiago Buitrago, and Heislermayer, another 22-year-old, was looking for his first ever pro win. He found Mathieu van der Poel and passed him. Meanwhile back in the group, Nibali distanced as was João Almeida. Landa made an attack. Carapaz and Hindley the only two riders who could follow whilst Wout Poels was doing a top domestique job. Their teammate found the front of the race closing that gap 1 and 1/2 minutes long and then going over the top and into the lead of the stage with 8 km to go. Heartbreak for Lennard Kämna who just couldn't hang on. He'd do his best to catch up on the descent but it was not to be happening. 8 km down the road, Buitrago was on his way to glory. One last try for Landa over the top as well but both Hindley and Carapaz were equal to do it. By this point with Almeida dropped, Landa was on his way onto the podium. Buitrago still had 25 seconds on Lennard Kämna. And once he got into the final few hundred meters, there was time, room, and the will to celebrate. A deserved celebration, one of perseverance and a story of superation. He got over the crash. He buried the tears of a second place just days before and Buitrago took the stage win. Second place going to Lennard Kämna after a brilliant ride. The bonus seconds had already been swept up by the time Carapaz and Hindley sprinted to the line. No gap between them but there were a few seconds put in to Mikel Landa who finished in the next little group. It was almost a minute put in to João Almeida and Vincenzo Nibali lost a little bit more time behind as well. He however stays in fifth place on the GC. The day belonged to Santiago Buitrago, 22-year-old from Bogota in Colombia. The youngest ever Colombian to win a Giro d'Italia stage. Buitrago beating Lennard Kämna Carapaz and then the GC guys. Carapaz and Hindley together with Mikel Landa finishing 7 seconds down. So stage 17, another day ticked off for the maglia rosa, but his lead still a very slim one. Just 3 seconds as they head into a day that should be for either the sprinters or the breakaway. Landa at 1:05, he's on the podium now and he has almost 50 seconds on João Almeida who's still in the game, but in fourth place and looking not quite as strong as the top trio in the mountains. Stage 18, a one-star stage in terms of GC action. It should be for the sprinters, but how are the legs? Will they make it? Will they control the breakaway? Days running out to win a stage, there will be plenty who want to be up the road. Again, tired legs will play a part. Treviso, the destination. Not too much climbing for a change and 152 km to be ridden. Stage up for grabs for either those fast men or stage hunters. As always, you're able to watch it live or on demand on GCN Plus, Discovery Plus, or on the television on Eurosport.
